mitre_attack: T1548.002 - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Bypass User Account Control
T1574.001 - Hijack Execution Flow: DLL Search Order Hijacking

threat.title: Windows UAC Bypass for Privilege Escalation via Registry Hijacking
threat.description: Windows User Account Control (UAC) can be bypassed through registry hijacking of auto-elevated executables like fodhelper.exe, eventvwr.exe, and sdclt.exe. Attackers exploit trusted Windows binaries that auto-elevate without UAC prompts to execute malicious code with administrative privileges. This allows ransomware, APTs, and malware to escalate from standard user to admin, disable security controls, and establish persistence.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker gains initial low-privilege access via phishing or exploit
2. Creates malicious registry key HKCU\Software\Classes\ms-settings\shell\open\command
3. Sets default value to point to malicious payload (e.g., C:\Temp\backdoor.exe)
4. Sets DelegateExecute value to empty string to bypass COM elevation
5. Executes fodhelper.exe (auto-elevated trusted Windows binary)
6. Fodhelper.exe reads attacker's registry key and executes payload with High integrity
7. Attacker gains SYSTEM/Administrator privileges without UAC prompt
8. Deploys ransomware, disables Windows Defender, creates admin accounts, establishes persistence
prerequisites: Low-privileged user access on Windows system
UAC set to default level or below (not 'Always Notify')
Auto-elevated Windows executables present (fodhelper.exe, eventvwr.exe, sdclt.exe)
Ability to modify HKEY_CURRENT_USER registry keys

cve_references.description: Windows Certificate Dialog UAC bypass allows elevation through help functionality
Windows UAC elevation of privilege vulnerability in WinSxS
Windows Installer elevation of privilege (InstallerFileTakeOver exploit)

real_world_breaches.impact: State-sponsored APT group used UAC bypass techniques including eventvwr.exe registry hijacking to escalate privileges in European government networks. Led to multi-year persistent access and data exfiltration.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Eventvwr.exe registry hijacking (HKCU\Software\Classes\mscfile\shell\open\command) allowed silent elevation to High integrity

real_world_breaches.impact: Ryuk ransomware operators used UAC bypass via sdclt.exe to escalate privileges before encryption. Resulted in $61 million in damages across healthcare and municipal targets in 2019.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Sdclt.exe registry hijacking (HKCU\Software\Classes\Folder\shell\open\command) combined with IFileOperation COM elevation

real_world_breaches.impact: Public release of UACME framework with 60+ UAC bypass methods enabled widespread privilege escalation by multiple threat actors including ransomware groups and cryptominers
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Open-source tool weaponizing fodhelper.exe, computerdefaults.exe, and DLL hijacking techniques

real_world_breaches.impact: Conti ransomware gang used fodhelper.exe UAC bypass to escalate privileges in Costa Rican government networks, leading to national emergency declaration and $30+ million in recovery costs
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Fodhelper.exe registry hijacking (HKCU\Software\Classes\ms-settings\shell\open\command) combined with Cobalt Strike beacon

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Windows UAC bypass via fodhelper.exe registry manipulation allowing elevation without prompt
UAC bypass through DLL hijacking of auto-elevated Windows Update components

mitigations.title: Enable AlwaysNotify UAC Setting and Registry Monitoring
mitigations.description: Set UAC to AlwaysNotify (highest setting) to prompt for all elevation attempts. Implement real-time registry monitoring for HKCU\Software\Classes keys commonly exploited in UAC bypass attacks.

code_example.code:
# Set UAC to AlwaysNotify (most secure setting)
Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System' -Name 'ConsentPromptBehaviorAdmin' -Value 2 -Type DWord
Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System' -Name 'ConsentPromptBehaviorUser' -Value 3 -Type DWord
Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System' -Name 'PromptOnSecureDesktop' -Value 1 -Type DWord

# Deploy via Group Policy:
# Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
# - "User Account Control: Behavior of the elevation prompt for administrators" = Prompt for consent on the secure desktop
# - "User Account Control: Detect application installations and prompt for elevation" = Enabled

# Monitor registry for UAC bypass attempts using PowerShell
$registryPaths = @(
    'HKCU:\Software\Classes\ms-settings\shell\open\command',
    'HKCU:\Software\Classes\mscfile\shell\open\command',
    'HKCU:\Software\Classes\Folder\shell\open\command',
    'HKCU:\Software\Classes\.exe\shell\open\command',
    'HKCU:\Environment'
)

foreach ($path in $registryPaths) {
    # Create registry watcher
    $watcher = [System.IO.FileSystemWatcher]::new()
    
    # Alternative: Use Process Monitor or Sysmon for production monitoring
    # This is a demonstration - use Sysmon Event ID 13 in production
    
    Write-Host "[INFO] Monitoring registry path: $path"
    
    if (Test-Path $path) {
        Write-Host "[ALERT] Suspicious registry key exists: $path" -ForegroundColor Red
        
        # Get registry values
        $values = Get-ItemProperty -Path $path -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
        
        # Log to Windows Event Log
        Write-EventLog -LogName 'Security' -Source 'UAC-Monitor' -EventId 5001 -EntryType Warning -Message "Potential UAC bypass detected: Registry key $path exists"
        
        # Optional: Delete malicious keys (only in response mode)
        # Remove-Item -Path $path -Recurse -Force
    }
}

# Production-grade monitoring with Sysmon (requires Sysmon installation)
# See detection section for Sysmon configuration

iso27001_controls: A.9.2.3 - Management of privileged access rights
validation_steps: Verify UAC setting: Get-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System' -Name ConsentPromptBehaviorAdmin
Test UAC prompt appears for all admin operations
Create test registry key in HKCU:\Software\Classes\ms-settings\shell\open\command
Verify monitoring alerts trigger
Confirm Sysmon Event ID 13 (Registry Set Value) logs to Event Viewer

mitigations.title: Implement AppLocker Application Whitelisting
mitigations.description: Use AppLocker to restrict execution of binaries to approved locations and publishers. Prevent execution from user-writable directories and block unsigned executables that could be used in privilege escalation chains.

code_example.code:
# Enable AppLocker service
Set-Service -Name AppIDSvc -StartupType Automatic
Start-Service -Name AppIDSvc

# Create AppLocker policy to block execution from temp directories
$rules = @()

# Allow execution from Windows and Program Files (signed by Microsoft)
$rules += New-AppLockerPolicy -RuleType Publisher -FilePublisher "*" -FilePath "C:\Windows\*" -Action Allow
$rules += New-AppLockerPolicy -RuleType Publisher -FilePublisher "*" -FilePath "C:\Program Files\*" -Action Allow
$rules += New-AppLockerPolicy -RuleType Publisher -FilePublisher "*" -FilePath "C:\Program Files (x86)\*" -Action Allow

# Block execution from common user-writable locations
$blockPaths = @(
    "C:\Users\*\AppData\Local\Temp\*",
    "C:\Windows\Temp\*",
    "C:\Temp\*",
    "%USERPROFILE%\Downloads\*"
)

foreach ($path in $blockPaths) {
    $rules += New-AppLockerPolicy -RuleType Path -Path $path -Action Deny -RuleNamePrefix "Block-UserWritable"
}

# Audit mode first (test before enforcement)
Set-AppLockerPolicy -PolicyObject $rules -Merge

# Export policy for Group Policy deployment
$policy = Get-AppLockerPolicy -Effective -Xml
$policy | Out-File -FilePath "C:\AppLockerPolicy.xml"

# Deploy via Group Policy:
# Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Application Control Policies > AppLocker

# Enhanced: Block specific UAC bypass executables from running with modified registry
$blockList = @(
    'fodhelper.exe',
    'eventvwr.exe',
    'sdclt.exe',
    'computerdefaults.exe',
    'SilentCleanup.exe'
)

# Monitor for suspicious parent-child process relationships
# Example: cmd.exe or powershell.exe spawned by fodhelper.exe
Write-Host "[INFO] Monitoring for suspicious process execution chains"

# Use with Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) for kernel-level enforcement
# WDAC provides stronger protection than AppLocker (cannot be bypassed by admin users)

nist_csf: PR.IP-1 - A baseline configuration is maintained
validation_steps: Verify AppLocker service is running: Get-Service AppIDSvc
Test execution of unsigned binary in C:\Temp (should be blocked)
Review AppLocker event logs: Event Viewer > Applications and Services > Microsoft > Windows > AppLocker
Test UAC bypass attempt (should fail due to execution restriction)
Deploy WDAC policy for enhanced protection

mitigations.title: Deploy Sysmon with UAC Bypass Detection Rules
mitigations.description: Install and configure Sysmon to monitor registry modifications, process creation, and file operations associated with UAC bypass techniques. Forward events to SIEM for centralized detection and response.

code_example.code:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<!-- Sysmon configuration for UAC bypass detection -->
<!-- Install: sysmon64.exe -accepteula -i sysmon-config.xml -->
<!-- Update: sysmon64.exe -c sysmon-config.xml -->
<Sysmon schemaversion="4.90">
  <EventFiltering>
    
    <!-- Event ID 1: Process Creation - Detect UAC bypass executables -->
    <ProcessCreate onmatch="include">
      <Image condition="end with">\fodhelper.exe</Image>
      <Image condition="end with">\eventvwr.exe</Image>
      <Image condition="end with">\sdclt.exe</Image>
      <Image condition="end with">\computerdefaults.exe</Image>
      <Image condition="end with">\SilentCleanup.exe</Image>
      <Image condition="end with">\slui.exe</Image>
      <Image condition="end with">\SystemPropertiesAdvanced.exe</Image>
    </ProcessCreate>
    
    <!-- Event ID 13: Registry Set Value - Detect UAC bypass registry modifications -->
    <RegistryEvent onmatch="include">
      <!-- Fodhelper.exe UAC bypass -->
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Software\Classes\ms-settings\shell\open\command</TargetObject>
      
      <!-- Eventvwr.exe UAC bypass -->
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Software\Classes\mscfile\shell\open\command</TargetObject>
      
      <!-- Sdclt.exe UAC bypass -->
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Software\Classes\Folder\shell\open\command</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Software\Classes\exefile\shell\open\command</TargetObject>
      
      <!-- Computerdefaults.exe UAC bypass -->
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Software\Classes\ms-settings\shell\open</TargetObject>
      
      <!-- Environment variable UAC bypass -->
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Environment\windir</TargetObject>
      <TargetObject condition="contains">\Environment\systemroot</TargetObject>
    </RegistryEvent>
    
    <!-- Event ID 7: DLL Load - Detect DLL hijacking in Windows directories -->
    <ImageLoad onmatch="include">
      <Image condition="begin with">C:\Windows\System32</Image>
      <ImageLoaded condition="begin with">C:\Users\</ImageLoaded>
    </ImageLoad>
    
    <!-- Event ID 11: File Creation - Detect DLL drops for hijacking -->
    <FileCreate onmatch="include">
      <TargetFilename condition="end with">.dll</TargetFilename>
      <TargetFilename condition="contains">C:\Windows\System32\</TargetFilename>
    </FileCreate>
    
  </EventFiltering>
</Sysmon>

<!-- PowerShell script to install and configure Sysmon -->
<!-- 
# Download Sysmon
Invoke-WebRequest -Uri "https://download.sysinternals.com/files/Sysmon.zip" -OutFile "C:\Temp\Sysmon.zip"
Expand-Archive -Path "C:\Temp\Sysmon.zip" -DestinationPath "C:\Temp\Sysmon"

# Save configuration above as sysmon-config.xml
# Install Sysmon with configuration
C:\Temp\Sysmon\sysmon64.exe -accepteula -i C:\Temp\sysmon-config.xml

# Verify installation
Get-Service Sysmon64
Get-WinEvent -LogName "Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational" -MaxEvents 10

# Configure Windows Event Forwarding to SIEM
# See detection section for SIEM queries
-->

nist_csf: DE.AE-3 - Event data are collected and correlated
DE.CM-7 - Monitoring for unauthorized activity is continuous
validation_steps: Install Sysmon: sysmon64.exe -accepteula -i config.xml
Verify service running: Get-Service Sysmon64
Test registry modification detection: Create test key in HKCU\Software\Classes\ms-settings\shell\open\command
Check Sysmon logs: Event Viewer > Applications and Services > Microsoft > Windows > Sysmon > Operational
Verify Event ID 13 logged with TargetObject containing suspicious registry path

mitigations.title: Implement Windows Defender Application Guard and Credential Guard
mitigations.description: Enable Windows Defender Application Guard for virtualization-based security and Credential Guard to protect against credential theft after privilege escalation.

code_example.code:
# Enable Windows Defender Application Guard (requires Enterprise edition and Hyper-V)
Enable-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online -FeatureName Windows-Defender-ApplicationGuard -All

# Enable Credential Guard via Group Policy or Registry
# Requires UEFI firmware, Secure Boot, TPM 2.0, and virtualization extensions

# Method 1: Enable via Registry
New-Item -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa' -Force
New-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa' -Name 'LsaCfgFlags' -Value 1 -PropertyType DWord -Force

# Method 2: Enable via Group Policy (recommended)
# Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > System > Device Guard
# - "Turn On Virtualization Based Security" = Enabled
# - "Credential Guard Configuration" = Enabled with UEFI lock

# Enable other Windows Defender features
Set-MpPreference -EnableControlledFolderAccess Enabled
Set-MpPreference -EnableNetworkProtection Enabled
Set-MpPreference -PUAProtection Enabled

# Configure Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) rules
# Block executable content from email and webmail
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids "BE9BA2D9-53EA-4CDC-84E5-9B1EEEE46550" -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled

# Block Office applications from creating executable content
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids "3B576869-A4EC-4529-8536-B80A7769E899" -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled

# Block credential stealing from LSASS (prevents post-exploitation)
Add-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids "9e6c4e1f-7d60-472f-ba1a-a39ef669e4b2" -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled

# Enable tamper protection (prevents attackers from disabling Defender)
Set-MpPreference -DisableTamperProtection $false

# Verify configuration
Get-MpComputerStatus
Get-MpPreference | Select-Object -Property *Guard*, *Protection*

# Check Credential Guard status
Get-CimInstance -ClassName Win32_DeviceGuard -Namespace root\Microsoft\Windows\DeviceGuard

# Expected output for SecurityServicesRunning:
# 1 = Credential Guard
# 2 = Hypervisor-enforced Code Integrity (HVCI)

Write-Host "[INFO] Credential Guard and virtualization-based security enabled" -ForegroundColor Green

nist_csf: PR.AC-4 - Access permissions and authorizations are managed
PR.PT-3 - Access to systems and assets is controlled
validation_steps: Check hardware requirements: TPM 2.0, UEFI, Secure Boot, VT-x/AMD-V
Enable Hyper-V: Enable-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online -FeatureName Microsoft-Hyper-V -All
Verify Credential Guard: Get-CimInstance -ClassName Win32_DeviceGuard -Namespace root\Microsoft\Windows\DeviceGuard
Confirm SecurityServicesRunning includes value 1 (Credential Guard)
Test that credential theft tools (mimikatz) are blocked from accessing LSASS

indicators: Sysmon Event ID 13: Registry value set in HKCU\Software\Classes\ms-settings\shell\open\command
Sysmon Event ID 1: fodhelper.exe, eventvwr.exe, or sdclt.exe spawning cmd.exe or powershell.exe
Process integrity level change from Medium to High without UAC prompt
Suspicious DLL loads from user-writable directories by Windows System32 executables
Registry keys created in HKCU\Software\Classes with shell\open\command subkeys
Execution of auto-elevated executables with modified registry context
Parent process integrity mismatch (Medium integrity parent, High integrity child)

log_queries.query:
// Detect UAC bypass via fodhelper.exe registry hijacking
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4688 // Process creation
| where NewProcessName endswith "\\fodhelper.exe" or 
        NewProcessName endswith "\\eventvwr.exe" or 
        NewProcessName endswith "\\sdclt.exe"
| join kind=inner (
    SecurityEvent
    | where EventID == 4657 // Registry modification
    | where ObjectName contains "\\Software\\Classes\\ms-settings\\shell\\open\\command" or
            ObjectName contains "\\Software\\Classes\\mscfile\\shell\\open\\command" or
            ObjectName contains "\\Software\\Classes\\Folder\\shell\\open\\command"
) on Computer, $left.SubjectUserSid == $right.SubjectUserSid
| where TimeGenerated between (datetime_add('minute', -5, TimeGenerated1) .. datetime_add('minute', 5, TimeGenerated1))
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, Account, NewProcessName, ObjectName, OperationType
| order by TimeGenerated desc

// Detect UAC bypass using Sysmon telemetry
SysmonEvent
| where EventID == 13 // Registry Set Value
| where TargetObject contains "\\Software\\Classes\\ms-settings\\shell\\open\\command" or
        TargetObject contains "\\Software\\Classes\\mscfile\\shell\\open\\command" or
        TargetObject contains "\\Software\\Classes\\Folder\\shell\\open\\command" or
        TargetObject contains "\\Software\\Classes\\exefile\\shell\\open\\command" or
        (TargetObject contains "\\Environment\\windir" and Details !contains "C:\\Windows")
| extend RegistryKey = tostring(split(TargetObject, '\\')[-1])
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, User, TargetObject, Details, Image
| join kind=inner (
    SysmonEvent
    | where EventID == 1 // Process creation
    | where Image endswith "\\fodhelper.exe" or 
            Image endswith "\\eventvwr.exe" or 
            Image endswith "\\sdclt.exe" or
            ParentImage endswith "\\fodhelper.exe" or
            ParentImage endswith "\\eventvwr.exe"
    | project TimeGenerated1=TimeGenerated, Computer, User, Image, ParentImage, CommandLine, IntegrityLevel
) on Computer, User
| where TimeGenerated1 between (TimeGenerated .. datetime_add('minute', 2, TimeGenerated))
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, User, TargetObject, Details, Image, ParentImage, CommandLine, IntegrityLevel
| order by TimeGenerated desc

index=windows sourcetype="WinEventLog:Sysmon" EventCode=13
(TargetObject="*\\Software\\Classes\\ms-settings\\shell\\open\\command*" OR
 TargetObject="*\\Software\\Classes\\mscfile\\shell\\open\\command*" OR
 TargetObject="*\\Software\\Classes\\Folder\\shell\\open\\command*" OR
 TargetObject="*\\Software\\Classes\\exefile\\shell\\open\\command*")
| eval registry_path=TargetObject
| join type=inner Computer, User
  [search index=windows sourcetype="WinEventLog:Sysmon" EventCode=1
   (Image="*\\fodhelper.exe" OR Image="*\\eventvwr.exe" OR Image="*\\sdclt.exe" OR
    ParentImage="*\\fodhelper.exe" OR ParentImage="*\\eventvwr.exe")
  | eval process_time=_time]
| where abs(process_time - _time) < 120
| table _time, Computer, User, registry_path, Details, Image, ParentImage, CommandLine
| sort -_time

event.code: 13 AND winlog.event_data.TargetObject: (*\\Software\\Classes\\ms-settings\\shell\\open\\command* OR *\\Software\\Classes\\mscfile\\shell\\open\\command* OR *\\Software\\Classes\\Folder\\shell\\open\\command*) OR
(event.code: 1 AND (process.name: (fodhelper.exe OR eventvwr.exe OR sdclt.exe) OR process.parent.name: (fodhelper.exe OR eventvwr.exe)))

# Assumes Windows logs forwarded to CloudWatch via CloudWatch Agent
fields @timestamp, @message
| filter eventID = 13 and targetObject like /Software\\Classes\\ms-settings\\shell\\open\\command/
| join
  (fields @timestamp as process_time, image, parentImage, commandLine
   | filter eventID = 1 and (image like /fodhelper.exe/ or image like /eventvwr.exe/ or parentImage like /fodhelper.exe/))
  on computerName
| filter abs(process_time - @timestamp) < 120000
| sort @timestamp desc
| limit 100
